---
title: "Succession - TV Show Report"
author: "Zuzanna Uljasz"
date: "May 2026"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
bibliography: citation.bib
---
<div style="margin-top: 60px;"></div>

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align="center", fig.cap="An image from *Succession*. [@succession_image]"}
knitr::include_graphics("succession_logo.jpg")
```

# Overview

*Succession* is an American drama series about the wealthy *Roy* family, owners of the fictional global media company *Waystar RoyCo*. After the health of family patriarch *Logan Roy* begins to decline, his children — *Connor*, *Kendall*, *Roman*, and *Shiv* — compete for influence, control, and power within the company [@succession_wikipedia].

# Viewership Statistics

The table below shows the number of episodes and the average viewership of *Succession* by season [@succession_wikipedia].

```{r}
viewership <- data.frame(
  Season = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
  Episodes = c(10, 10, 9, 10),
  Release_Date = c("June 3, 2018", "August 11, 2019", "October 17, 2021", "March 26, 2023"),
  Average_Viewership_Mln = c(0.603, 0.597, 0.553, 0.705),
  Rotten_Tomatoes_Score = c(89, 97, 97, 97),
  Metacritic_Score = c(70, 89, 92, 92)
)

knitr::kable(
  viewership,
  caption = "Viewership and ratings statistics for *Succession*",
  col.names = c(
    "Season",
    "Episodes",
    "Release date",
    "Average viewership (mln)",
    "Rotten Tomatoes score",
    "Metacritic score"
  ),
  align = "c"
)
```

```{r}
mean_viewership <- mean(viewership$Average_Viewership_Mln)
max_viewership <- max(viewership$Average_Viewership_Mln)
min_viewership <- min(viewership$Average_Viewership_Mln)

mean_rotten <- mean(viewership$Rotten_Tomatoes_Score)
mean_metacritic <- mean(viewership$Metacritic_Score)

```

The average viewership of *Succession* was **`r round(mean_viewership, 3)`** million viewers. The average *Rotten Tomatoes* score was **`r round(mean_rotten, 1)`**. The average *Metacritic* score was **`r round(mean_metacritic, 1)`**. The highest average viewership was **`r max_viewership`** million viewers, while the lowest was **`r min_viewership`** million viewers.


## Viewership Over Time

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.width=8, fig.height=4.8, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(viewership, aes(x = Season, y = Average_Viewership_Mln)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9, color = "#2C3E50") +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, color = "#2C3E50") +
  geom_label(
    aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", Average_Viewership_Mln)),
    vjust = -0.8,
    size = 3.8,
    label.size = 0,
    fill = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = viewership$Season
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(0.50, 0.75, 0.05),
    limits = c(0.50, 0.75)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Viewership of Succession",
    subtitle = "Average U.S. viewers per episode by season, in millions",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Viewers, millions",
    caption = "Source: Wikipedia"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 10, 10)
  )
```

## Episode-to-episode viewership

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)

episode_viewership <- data.frame(
  Overall = 1:39,
  Season = c(rep(1, 10), rep(2, 10), rep(3, 9), rep(4, 10)),
  Episode = c(1:10, 1:10, 1:9, 1:10),
  Viewers_Mln = c(
    0.582, 0.491, 0.605, 0.543, 0.583, 0.673, 0.626, 0.637, 0.558, 0.730,
    0.612, 0.603, 0.607, 0.577, 0.507, 0.610, 0.508, 0.579, 0.705, 0.660,
    0.564, 0.520, 0.405, 0.484, 0.584, 0.525, 0.645, 0.613, 0.634,
    0.598, 0.481, 0.609, 0.695, 0.652, 0.847, 0.739, 0.746, 0.789, 0.896
  )
)

episode_viewership$Change_Mln <- c(NA, diff(episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln))
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
ggplot(episode_viewership[-1, ], aes(x = Overall, y = Change_Mln, fill = Change_Mln > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(10.5, 20.5, 29.5), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(2, 10, 20, 29, 39),
    labels = c("Ep. 2", "Ep. 10", "Ep. 20", "Ep. 29", "Ep. 39")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Episode-to-Episode Changes in Succession Viewership",
    subtitle = "Positive bars show increases; negative bars show decreases",
    x = "Episode order",
    y = "Change in viewers, millions",
    caption = "Source: Wikipedia"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
first_episode <- episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln[1]
final_episode <- episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln[39]
overall_change <- final_episode - first_episode

lowest_viewership <- min(episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln)
lowest_episode <- episode_viewership$Overall[which.min(episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln)]

highest_viewership <- max(episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln)
highest_episode <- episode_viewership$Overall[which.max(episode_viewership$Viewers_Mln)]

largest_increase <- max(episode_viewership$Change_Mln, na.rm = TRUE)
largest_increase_episode <- episode_viewership$Overall[which.max(episode_viewership$Change_Mln)]

largest_decrease <- min(episode_viewership$Change_Mln, na.rm = TRUE)
largest_decrease_episode <- episode_viewership$Overall[which.min(episode_viewership$Change_Mln)]
```

The viewership of *Succession* increased overall from **`r first_episode`** million viewers in the first episode to **`r final_episode`** million viewers in the final episode. This was an increase of **`r round(overall_change, 3)`** million viewers.

The lowest viewership was recorded in episode **`r lowest_episode`**, with **`r lowest_viewership`** million viewers, while the highest viewership was recorded in episode **`r highest_episode`**, with **`r highest_viewership`** million viewers.

The largest episode-to-episode increase happened before episode **`r largest_increase_episode`**, when viewership rose by **`r round(largest_increase, 3)`** million viewers. The largest episode-to-episode decrease happened before episode **`r largest_decrease_episode`**, when viewership changed by **`r round(largest_decrease, 3)`** million viewers.

# AI Usage

AI was used for minor text formatting and visualization, including improving the appearance of plots, hiding code chunks in the rendered report, and better rewording of the text content. AI was also used to help speed up the process of preparing the viewership data table.

# Bibliography


<div class="tocify-extend-page" data-unique="tocify-extend-page" style="height: 0;"></div>
